title: Accessioning
subtitle: The intake door for surgical specimens
overview: The intake desk for the entire surgical pathology service. Specimens arrive from operating rooms, clinics, and outside hospitals; each gets logged into the LIS, given a unique case number, and routed next door to grossing. Chain-of-custody starts here.

title: Grossing
subtitle: Multiple stations · description and dissection
overview: Right next door to accessioning. Pathologists' assistants and residents work at four grossing stations, each with its own downdraft hood and sink. Specimens are measured, photographed, inked at margins, and submitted as small tissue cassettes. How a specimen is grossed often determines whether the diagnosis can be made downstream.

title: Molecular Diagnostics
subtitle: DNA · RNA · the molecular signature
overview: Where DNA and RNA do the talking. PCR, sequencing, FISH, and microarray-based assays detect mutations, fusions, and copy-number changes — most of which directly drive cancer treatment decisions today. Targeted therapy in oncology starts with a result from this lab.

title: Histology · Processing
subtitle: Tissue cassettes → paraffin blocks
overview: Cassettes go in here at the end of the day and emerge as paraffin-embedded blocks the next morning. Overnight, automated tissue processors run them through graded alcohols, xylene, and molten paraffin so they can be sectioned for microscopy.
tests: Routine processing for nearly every surgical specimen
Rapid runs for small biopsies and frozens

title: Histology · Microtomy
subtitle: Blocks → glass slides
overview: Where blocks become slides. Microtome stations shave 4-µm ribbons off the embedded paraffin block; ribbons get floated on warm water and lifted onto charged glass slides. The slides you've stared at all year were made here.
tests: Recuts for IHC and special stains
methods: Warm water bath (~40 °C) for ribbon flotation

title: Histology · Collation
subtitle: Stain · coverslip · deliver
overview: The last stop in histology. Slides are stained with H&E, coverslipped, paired with paperwork, sorted by case, and delivered to the pathologist's queue. If a slide is missing at sign-out, this is where the search begins.
methods: Slide barcoding tied to case accession

title: Slide Scanning
subtitle: Glass → digital images
overview: Whole-slide scanners image entire slides at 20× or 40× and produce gigabyte-scale digital files. Telepathology, AI-assisted screening, and educational archives all start here. Increasingly, this is also where the day starts for pathologists who sign out fully digitally.

title: IHC Staining
subtitle: Antibody-based protein detection
overview: Immunohistochemistry uses antibodies to detect specific proteins in tissue. IHC is the workhorse of modern surgical pathology — it's how pathologists distinguish melanoma from carcinoma, find an unknown primary, classify lymphomas, and quantify HER2 to guide breast cancer therapy.

title: Microbiology · Walk-through
subtitle: Then cross the bridge to the Core Lab
overview: On our way back from IHC we pass through Microbiology — biosafety cabinets for working with cultures, MALDI-TOF for organism ID from colonies, automated culture instruments, and serology platforms. Most ID consults start with results from this floor. From here, the sky bridge takes us across to the Core Lab in the main hospital.
methods: MALDI-TOF for organism ID from colonies

title: Core Laboratory · Overview
subtitle: The 24/7 STAT lab · ~80% of clinical tests
overview: This is the 24/7 STAT lab — the largest single room in the department. The conveyor track running down the room is a Beckman Coulter automation line: tubes arrive by pneumatic tube, get centrifuged, decapped, sampled by analyzers in series, then recapped and stored. One sample feeds hematology, coagulation, immunoassay and chemistry analyzers in parallel. Volume and turnaround are the metrics: chemistry ~30 min, troponin under an hour, CBC under 30 min.
tests: TSH, free T4, βHCG, PSA, drug levels
methods: Sample tracking via barcoded tubes on conveyor

title: Surgical Pathology · Sign-Out
subtitle: 16 individual sign-out offices · where the diagnosis lives
overview: This is the answer at the end of every surgical specimen's journey. Pathologists examine glass slides under the microscope at one of 16 individual sign-out offices and put their name on the diagnosis. Almost every cancer diagnosis you've read in a chart was signed out from a desk like one of these — slides, microscope, monitor for the digital queue, dictation system into the LIS.
methods: Light microscopy on H&E and special stains
Digital sign-out queues in the LIS
Multi-headed teaching scopes for tumor boards

title: Transfusion · Blood Bank
subtitle: If we have time — last stop on the tour
overview: Where blood is typed, screened, crossmatched, and dispensed for transfusion. This is also where transfusion reactions get worked up and apheresis (e.g. plasma exchange for TTP) is coordinated. The clock here matters: trauma cases are measured in minutes. If we have time at the end of the tour, we'll come up here.
